ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Singaporean economic beehoon with luncheon meat and fried egg; mapped to rice vermicelli dish as closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers cooked rice noodles as a base; no specific composite code exists for this Singaporean hawker preparation with luncheon meat and fried egg.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite hawker dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker or canteen plate of economic beehoon with vegetables, luncheon meat and fried egg is approximately 300 g, providing around 510 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Economic Beehoon (Other Vegetables, Luncheon Meat, Fried Egg) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Economic Beehoon (Other Vegetables, Luncheon Meat, Fried Egg), CR ID F05060049. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this primary regional source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice noodles / rice vermicelli analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite Singaporean hawker dish. GI of 61 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles / rice vermicelli (GI ~61, medium category), which forms the dominant carbohydrate base of this dish. The presence of fat (8.3 g/100g) and protein (4.19 g/100g) from luncheon meat and egg will moderately attenuate the glycaemic response relative to plain rice vermicelli. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 58.23) / 100 ≈ 35.5, rounded to 35.6, based on 300 g serving size providing 58.23 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 481 mg/100g — significantly exceeds the 400 mg/100g threshold; driven by luncheon meat and soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (481 mg/100g); if consumed, request preparation without luncheon meat and with minimal soy sauce, and restrict portion to no more than 150 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined rice vermicelli base with medium-high GI (~61) and low dietary fibre (1.33 g/100g) promotes rapid postprandial glucose elevation; saturated fat from luncheon meat further impairs insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in small portions (≤150 g) infrequently, substitute luncheon meat with lean protein such as steamed fish or tofu, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (481 mg/100g) combined with saturated fat from deep-fried luncheon meat and cooking oil elevates both blood pressure and atherogenic lipid risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to its combined sodium and saturated fat load; if consumed, choose a reduced-sodium preparation without luncheon meat and limit serving to 150 g or less.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Processed pork luncheon meat contributes sodium nitrates and saturated fat that are pro-inflammatory; high sodium (481 mg/100g) worsens RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid luncheon meat in this dish due to its pro-inflammatory processed pork content and high sodium; substitute with oily fish rich in omega-3 fatty acids and request reduced-sodium preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Economic Beehoon with Vegetables, Luncheon Meat and Fried Egg is a popular Singaporean hawker and school canteen dish consisting of stir-fried rice vermicelli (beehoon) served with a combination of mixed vegetables, deep-fried luncheon meat slices, and a pan-fried egg. The dish is a staple of Singapore's 'economic rice/noodle' culture, where diners select affordable side dishes to accompany a carbohydrate base at low cost. The rice vermicelli provides a soft, slightly chewy texture and a neutral flavour that absorbs the savoury seasoning of soy sauce and cooking oil used during stir-frying. Luncheon meat (typically a canned processed pork product) is deep-fried until golden and crispy, contributing a salty, umami-rich flavour and a contrasting crunchy exterior. The fried egg adds richness and additional protein. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 170 kcal, 4.19 g protein, 8.3 g fat, 19.41 g carbohydrate, 1.33 g dietary fibre, and 481 mg sodium. The high sodium content (481 mg/100g) is a primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The refined carbohydrate base with medium GI (~61) and low fibre makes blood glucose management challenging for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The processed luncheon meat contributes saturated fat and sodium nitrates, raising concerns for cardiovascular and inflammatory conditions. This dish is not suitable for vegetarian, vegan, or halal diets due to the pork-based luncheon meat. It is commonly consumed at breakfast or lunch in hawker centres, coffee shops, and school canteens across Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in economic beehoon with luncheon meat and egg?
Can diabetics eat beehoon with luncheon meat?
Is beehoon with luncheon meat high in sodium?
Is economic beehoon suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05060049), which is the primary approved regional source for Singaporean dishes. All seven reported nutrient fields are exact matches from this canonical source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for rice vermicelli, which is the dominant carbohydrate component.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB source for this composite dish): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrients, and moisture data for this composite hawker preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05060049) as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat estimated at 2.1 g/100g based on typical luncheon meat and egg contribution within the reported total fat of 8.3 g/100g, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for similar composite dishes; this field should be updated when HPB publishes a direct value. Cultural correction applied: input record incorrectly tagged primary_cuisine as 'western' and dietary_tags as 'vegetarian'/'plant_based'; this dish is Singaporean cuisine and is not vegetarian due to pork luncheon meat and egg. Halal status set to false as standard luncheon meat contains pork. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for rice vermicelli; no direct composite-dish GI measurement found in approved sources.
